title: Tailoring flake size and chemistry to improve impact resistance of graphene oxide thin films
abstract: Multilayered graphene oxide (MLGO) possesses high elastic modulus and strength, as well as enhanced resistance to inter-layer sliding from functional groups. This makes it a promising candidate for impact-resistant thin films and nanocomposites. However, the effects functionalization and flake size on the physics of MLGO failure under high-strain rate impact remains to be fully understood. While laser induced projectile impact tests (LIPIT) have enabled microballistic characterization of MLGO, the dynamics of interlayer sliding processes cannot be accurately studied in physical experiments due to resolution challenges of existing techniques. Here we utilize systematically coarse-grained molecular dynamics to simulate MLGO under high-rate microballistic impact, explicitly accounting for functional group distributions while approaching micron-size specimen dimensions. In doing so, we evaluate the effects of oxidation level on the thin films' ability to dissipate ballistic energy. We additionally explore effects of varying flake size on these systems, improving on studies that only consider infinite flake dimensions. The primary failure mechanism of these thin films (consisting of relatively small flakes) is found to be sliding of the GO flakes as no bonds were broken. We discover that elevated levels of oxidation increase the amount of energy required to penetrate the film, as do larger GO flake sizes as they increase energy dissipation via friction between sliding flakes. These findings illustrate the potential for MLGO films to be tailored for maximum ballistic energy dissipation, providing a starting point to design impact-resistant GO materials for diverse applications.
results: A detailed visualization of the LIPIT setup is shown in Fig. 2, where the CG beads in the GO film are color-coded by relative out-of-plane (z) position. This depiction reveals the rippled texture of the GO film, which is consistent with experimental findings. Despite bond-breaking being enabled in all simulations, no bonds were broken upon impact. Increased oxidation levels are known to weaken GO sheets, but even highly oxidized GO has an ultimate tensile stress of over 50 GPa. The specific penetration energies of the GO films are consistent with those measured experimentally for unoxidized graphene films, generally falling between 0.2 and 0.7 MJ/kg. The average specific penetration energy of systems with large, defect-free flakes was found to be 5.19 MJ/kg, indicating that larger flakes fail via bond breakage.
discussion: At first glance, it appears that larger impact velocities increase specific penetration energy; however, when normalized by impact velocity squared, the resulting values decrease as impact velocity increases, indicating a decrease in film impact resistance with faster impact velocities. This effect becomes more pronounced for larger flake sizes, suggesting that the mobility of the GO flakes decreases with higher impact velocities. Increasing the films' oxide content generally increases penetration energy at low impact velocities, but this trend diminishes with increasing impact velocity. The mobility of GO flakes affects the films' penetration energy, as they can adjust their positions to redistribute the load from the incoming bullet, especially at lower impact velocities.
conclusion: In this work, coarse-grained molecular dynamics is applied to uncover critical elements to the design of multilayer graphene oxide films for use as ballistic protection materials. Because all GO films failed via sliding of individual flakes, shear forces dictate the penetration resistance of systems within this works' parameter space. Stronger nonbonded interactions increase shear resistance, and factors such as oxidation level and flake size generally increase penetration resistance. However, the ability of flakes to redistribute load is affected by their mobility, which decreases with higher oxidation levels and larger flake sizes. These findings provide foundational information to design ballistic protection materials for maximum effect.
